title: A Framework for Management Information Systems
relevance_summary: Introduced the foundational taxonomy of structured, semi-structured, and unstructured decisions and coined the term 'Decision Support System', establishing the core rationale for computer-based executive decision intelligence. ESS builds directly on this framing of how systems should augment human judgment for unstructured, executive-level decisions.

title: The Impact of Executive Information Systems on Organizational Design, Intelligence, and Decision Making
relevance_summary: Empirically examined how computer-based executive information systems affect senior managers' decision processes and organizational intelligence, finding that accessible synthesized information significantly improves perceived decision quality. Directly motivates ESS's goal of delivering AI-processed signal intelligence to executive audiences.

title: Past, Present, and Future of Decision Support Technology
venue: Decision Support Systems, Vol. 33, No. 2, pp. 111-126
relevance_summary: Traced DSS evolution from isolated individual tools to web-enabled, inter-organizational systems integrating heterogeneous data sources, identifying the trend toward richer, more automated decision intelligence. Positions ESS as the next stage of DSS evolution incorporating NLP and AI for signal synthesis.

title: Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI): Concepts, Taxonomies, Opportunities and Challenges toward Responsible AI
relevance_summary: Provides the most comprehensive taxonomy of XAI methods and defines explainability requirements differentiated by audience type, framing explainability as a prerequisite for responsible organizational AI deployment. ESS must produce executive-interpretable outputs, making this taxonomy foundational to its output design.

title: Why Should I Trust You?: Explaining the Predictions of Any Classifier
venue: Proceedings of the 22nd ACM SIGKDD International Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining, pp. 1135-1144
relevance_summary: Introduced LIME, demonstrating that opaque model outputs can be made interpretable to non-technical stakeholders. ESS relies on analogous post-hoc explanation mechanisms to make AI-synthesized signal outputs legible and trustworthy for executives.

title: Explanation in Artificial Intelligence: Insights from the Social Sciences
relevance_summary: Synthesizes cognitive and social science research on what constitutes a satisfying explanation for human decision-makers, arguing that AI explanations must be contrastive, selective, and socially situated. This grounds ESS's explanation layer in human-centered design principles for executive consumers.

title: Sensemaking in Organizations
relevance_summary: Defined organizational sensemaking as the process by which actors construct plausible interpretations of equivocal environmental signals, establishing that organizations process cues rather than raw data. ESS computationally instantiates this sensemaking process — automating cue extraction, frame construction, and equivocality reduction.

title: Organizational Information Requirements, Media Richness and Structural Design
relevance_summary: Formally modeled organizational information processing as serving two functions: reducing uncertainty and resolving equivocality. Demonstrated that a primary managerial problem is lack of clarity rather than lack of data — directly the problem ESS addresses.

title: Making Sense of Sensemaking 2: A Macrocognitive Model
venue: IEEE Intelligent Systems, Vol. 21, No. 5, pp. 88-92
relevance_summary: Proposed the data-frame theory of sensemaking, modeling the bidirectional relationship between incoming organizational signals and cognitive frames that interpret them. This cognitive architecture directly informs how ESS structures signal ingestion, frame generation, and pattern-handling pipelines.

title: Multi-Source Knowledge Fusion: A Survey
venue: World Wide Web, Vol. 23, No. 4, pp. 2567-2592
relevance_summary: Comprehensively surveyed methods for fusing knowledge from heterogeneous sources including multi-modal fusion, cross-knowledge-graph alignment, and collaborative reasoning — directly applicable to ESS's signal integration and synthesis layer.

title: Knowledge Graph-Driven Data Processing for Business Intelligence
venue: WIREs Data Mining and Knowledge Discovery, Vol. 14
relevance_summary: Presents a knowledge graph-centered framework for integrating structured and unstructured enterprise data sources into actionable business intelligence using NLP for signal extraction from text streams. Directly relevant to ESS's architecture for ingesting heterogeneous organizational signals.

title: Big Data Integration
venue: Synthesis Lectures on Data Management, Morgan and Claypool Publishers
relevance_summary: Systematically addresses schema alignment, record linkage, and data fusion across heterogeneous structured and unstructured data sources, providing the technical foundation for combining disparate organizational signals into a unified representation — foundational to ESS's normalization pipeline.
